hostControl.intro: Manage the printer's Linux host — check its health and OS state. Services, disk cleanup and system settings arrive in later phases.

help.title: What is this?
help.body: FilaMind runs on the printer's small Linux computer (the host). This panel reads that machine's live health and OS state so you can spot problems — an overheating CPU, a full disk, an unsynced clock — without opening a terminal.
help.step1: Read the cards for CPU temperature, memory, disk and network at a glance.
help.step2: Watch the top-processes table to see what is using the CPU right now.
help.step3: Leave auto-refresh on for a live view, or turn it off and refresh manually.

monitor.title: Reading the monitor
monitor.body: Everything here is read-only. Bars turn amber past 75% and red past 90%. CPU temperature turns amber at 70 °C and red at 80 °C. The throttle warning only appears on boards that report it (mainly Raspberry Pi).

host.def: The small Linux computer (e.g. a Raspberry Pi or BTT CB1) that runs Klipper, Moonraker and FilaMind — separate from the printer's microcontrollers.

load.def: How many processes were waiting to run, averaged over 1 / 5 / 15 minutes. Roughly: a value near the core count means the CPU is busy; well above it means it is overloaded.

swap.def: Disk space used as overflow when RAM fills up. Heavy swap use slows the host down — a sign it is short on memory.

throttle.def: When the board reduces speed to protect itself — usually from an under-powered supply (under-voltage) or overheating. Reported mainly on Raspberry Pi.

ntp.def: Network Time Protocol keeps the host clock accurate. An unsynced clock can break timelapses, logs and TLS certificates.

locale.def: The host's language and keyboard layout settings, used by the OS for text, dates and the console keymap.
